content.text: Data Migration Rollback Plans

Data migration rollback plans define how to undo a database schema change, a large-scale data backfill, or a storage structure change if the migration causes a production regression. Unlike application code rollbacks, data migration rollbacks carry the risk of data loss or data inconsistency — the plan must account for writes that arrived after the migration ran but before the rollback executes.

Before any production data migration, classify it by reversibility. The reversibility classification determines whether a rollback plan is a simple script, a snapshot restore, or not possible at all. Never run a migration without knowing which category it belongs to.

This sub-subtopic covers the reversibility spectrum for database migrations, the expand/contract pattern for blue-green-safe schema changes, down migration scripts with the Alembic migration framework, snapshot-based recovery for irreversible migrations, testing rollback plans in staging, and the special case of Claude API usage-log and conversation-history migrations.

attrs.alt: Data migration reversibility spectrum: irreversible (snapshot restore) to trivially reversible (DROP new object)

content.text: Reversibility Classification
Before writing a migration, classify it on the reversibility spectrum. This determines which rollback strategy is available and how long recovery takes.
Category
Migration Type
Rollback Method
TTR
Data Loss Risk
Trivially reversible
ADD nullable column, CREATE TABLE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
DROP the new object
None — the new object held no authoritative data yet
Expand/contract
ADD column + dual-write period, then contract
Stop new writes; DROP new column
None if rollback before contract phase completes
Constrained
ADD NOT NULL column, RENAME column, FK constraint, backfill transforms
Down migration script reverting the change
Low — down migration must handle rows written under new schema
Irreversible
DROP TABLE/COLUMN, DELETE rows, TRUNCATE, type narrowing
Restore from pre-migration snapshot
Possible — writes after snapshot are lost
The Expand/Contract Pattern

Expand/contract is the safest pattern for schema changes that need to be compatible with a blue-green deployment. It splits a breaking schema change into three non-breaking phases that can each be independently rolled back.
Phase 1: Expand (backward-compatible add)
Add the new column (nullable, no default — zero impact on existing queries)
Deploy application code that WRITES to both old and new columns
New reads still come from the old column (backward compatible)
Phase 2: Backfill (populate new column)
Backfill the new column for existing rows (batched to avoid lock contention)
Application still writes to both columns
Once backfill complete, reads can migrate to the new column

# backfill_model_version.py — batched backfill with rollback safety
import psycopg2, time

conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn=PROD_DB_URL)

BATCH_SIZE = 1000
SLEEP_MS = 50   # throttle to avoid I/O saturation

def backfill():
    last_id = 0
    while True:
        with conn.cursor() as cur:
            cur.execute("""
                UPDATE conversation_logs
                SET model_version_v2 = model_version || '-migrated'
                WHERE id > %s AND model_version_v2 IS NULL
                ORDER BY id
                LIMIT %s
                RETURNING id
            """, (last_id, BATCH_SIZE))
            rows = cur.fetchall()
            conn.commit()

        if not rows:
            print("Backfill complete.")
            break
        last_id = rows[-1][0]
        print(f"Backfilled up to id={last_id} ({len(rows)} rows)")
        time.sleep(SLEEP_MS / 1000)

backfill()
Rollback During Expand/Contract
Rollback is safe at any point before the contract phase removes the old column. The rollback procedure is: stop writing to the new column, then drop it.
Phase 3: Contract (remove old column)

The contract phase is the point of no return. Once the old column is dropped, rollback requires a snapshot restore. Do not contract until the new column has been the sole read source for at least one full release cycle with no incidents.
Down Migrations with Alembic
For the constrained reversibility category, the rollback plan is a down migration script that the migration framework (Alembic for Python services) applies in the reverse direction.
Writing Reversible Alembic Migrations

# migrations/versions/a1b2c3_add_token_budget_column.py
"""Add token_budget column to api_requests"""
from alembic import op
import sqlalchemy as sa

revision = 'a1b2c3'
down_revision = '9z8y7x'
branch_labels = None
depends_on = None

def upgrade():
    op.add_column(
        'api_requests',
        sa.Column('token_budget', sa.Integer(), nullable=True, default=None)
    )
    # Backfill: default to max_tokens for existing rows
    op.execute("""
        UPDATE api_requests
        SET token_budget = max_tokens
        WHERE token_budget IS NULL AND max_tokens IS NOT NULL
    """)

def downgrade():
    # Explicit downgrade: simply remove the column
    # Rows written with token_budget values will have that data lost,
    # but token_budget is additive — no downstream systems depend on it yet.
    op.drop_column('api_requests', 'token_budget')
Running Down Migrations

# Check current migration head
alembic current

# Run down one step (to previous revision)
DATABASE_URL=$PROD_DB_URL alembic downgrade -1

# Run down to a specific revision
DATABASE_URL=$PROD_DB_URL alembic downgrade 9z8y7x

# Verify schema after downgrade
DATABASE_URL=$PROD_DB_URL alembic current
psql $PROD_DB_URL -c "\d api_requests"   # confirm column is gone
When Downgrade Is Not Safe
A downgrade is unsafe when rows written under the new schema contain data that the old schema cannot represent. Always check for this before running downgrade in production.

# pre_downgrade_check.py — confirm downgrade is safe before executing
import psycopg2

conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn=PROD_DB_URL)

def check_safe_to_downgrade(table: str, new_column: str) -> bool:
    """
    A downgrade that drops `new_column` is safe only if no downstream
    consumer has been reading from it in production yet.
    Check: are any rows in the table where only new_column has data
    and the old column is NULL?
    """
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute(f"""
            SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {table}
            WHERE {new_column} IS NOT NULL AND model_version IS NULL
        """)
        count = cur.fetchone()[0]
    if count > 0:
        print(f"UNSAFE: {count} rows have only new column populated. "
              f"Downgrade will lose data. Use snapshot restore instead.")
        return False
    print(f"Safe to downgrade: no rows exclusively in new column.")
    return True

check_safe_to_downgrade("api_requests", "token_budget")
Snapshot-Based Recovery for Irreversible Migrations

When a migration cannot be reversed with a down script (DROP TABLE, DELETE, TRUNCATE), the rollback plan is to restore from a pre-migration snapshot. This is slower and loses writes made after the snapshot.
Pre-Migration Snapshot Protocol
Create an RDS snapshot immediately before running the migration (not the daily backup — a dedicated snapshot with the migration ticket ID in the name)
Record the snapshot ID and creation timestamp in the migration PR and the incident runbook
Do not proceed with the migration until the snapshot shows status 'available'
Set a 7-day retention on the snapshot (default RDS retention may be shorter)

#!/bin/bash
# pre_migration_snapshot.sh
MIGRATION_ID="$1"   # e.g. "INC-20240410-drop-legacy-logs"
DB_INSTANCE="claude-api-prod"

# Create snapshot
SNAPSHOT_ID="pre-migration-${MIGRATION_ID}-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M)"
aws rds create-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier "$DB_INSTANCE" \
  --db-snapshot-identifier "$SNAPSHOT_ID" \
  --tags Key=migration_id,Value="$MIGRATION_ID" \
         Key=ttl_days,Value=7

# Wait for snapshot to be available (typically 2-5 min)
echo "Waiting for snapshot $SNAPSHOT_ID to be available..."
aws rds wait db-snapshot-available --db-snapshot-identifier "$SNAPSHOT_ID"
echo "Snapshot ready: $SNAPSHOT_ID"

# Record in migration log
echo "{"snapshot_id": "$SNAPSHOT_ID", "migration": "$MIGRATION_ID", "created_at": "$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ)"}" \
  >> migration-snapshots.log
Snapshot Restore Procedure

#!/bin/bash
# restore_from_snapshot.sh — use only when down migration is not possible
SNAPSHOT_ID="$1"   # from migration-snapshots.log
DB_INSTANCE="claude-api-prod"
RESTORE_INSTANCE="claude-api-prod-restored"

# Step 1: Restore to a new instance (keeps prod running while restore completes)
aws rds restore-db-instance-from-db-snapshot \
  --db-instance-identifier "$RESTORE_INSTANCE" \
  --db-snapshot-identifier "$SNAPSHOT_ID" \
  --db-instance-class db.r6g.4xlarge \
  --no-publicly-accessible

# Wait for restore (typically 15-30 min for large databases)
aws rds wait db-instance-available --db-instance-identifier "$RESTORE_INSTANCE"
echo "Restore instance ready"

# Step 2: Verify data integrity on restored instance
psql "$(get_endpoint $RESTORE_INSTANCE)" -c "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM conversation_logs;"
# Compare row count with pre-migration baseline from monitoring

# Step 3: Promote restored instance (DNS flip)
# Update parameter store with new connection string
aws ssm put-parameter \
  --name /claude-api/prod/db-url \
  --value "postgresql://user:pass@$(get_endpoint $RESTORE_INSTANCE)/claude" \
  --overwrite

# Step 4: Rolling restart of application pods to pick up new DB URL
kubectl rollout restart deployment/claude-gateway -n claude-api
kubectl rollout restart deployment/claude-serving -n claude-api
Quantifying Data Loss Window
When restoring from a snapshot, writes between snapshot creation and restore completion are lost. Calculate and document this window before deciding to proceed with snapshot restore.

# estimate_data_loss.py — quantify writes lost during snapshot restore
from datetime import datetime
import psycopg2

def estimate_lost_writes(snapshot_created_at: datetime, restore_started_at: datetime):
    conn = psycopg2.connect(dsn=PROD_DB_URL)
    with conn.cursor() as cur:
        cur.execute("""
            SELECT
                COUNT(*) AS rows_at_risk,
                MIN(created_at) AS earliest,
                MAX(created_at) AS latest
            FROM conversation_logs
            WHERE created_at BETWEEN %s AND %s
        """, (snapshot_created_at, restore_started_at))
        result = cur.fetchone()
    rows, earliest, latest = result
    print(f"Rows at risk of loss: {rows:,}")
    print(f"Time window: {earliest} → {latest}")
    print(f"These rows will NOT be in the restored instance.")
    return rows
Testing Migration Rollback Plans

Migration rollback plans must be tested on a production-scale data clone before the migration runs in production. Testing on staging with synthetic data misses lock contention, replication lag, and performance regressions that only appear at scale.
Production Clone Testing Protocol
Create a production clone: restore the latest production RDS snapshot to a fresh instance tagged 'test-clone'
Run the migration on the clone; measure wall-clock time and lock wait events
Execute the rollback plan immediately after migration completes (don't wait to verify — simulate the incident scenario)
Measure TTR on the clone; compare against TTR target in the runbook
If TTR exceeds target: optimize the down migration script or the snapshot restore path before running in production
Migration Checklist
Migration classified on reversibility spectrum (irreversible / constrained / expand-contract / trivial)
Down migration script written and syntax-checked
Pre-migration snapshot script tested
Data loss window estimated for worst case
Rollback tested on production clone; TTR recorded
Application rollback order confirmed (app first, then DB — never DB first)
Downstream consumers of migrated tables notified
Maintenance window scheduled if migration holds locks > 1s on high-traffic tables
Claude API-Specific Migration Considerations
The Claude API database contains several tables with particular migration sensitivities:
Table
Write Rate
Sensitivity
Migration Approach
conversation_logs
Very high (every API call)
Cannot lock; any schema change must be zero-downtime
Expand/contract only; never ADD NOT NULL without default
api_usage_billing
High (every metered call)
Financial data; data loss directly affects billing accuracy
Always snapshot before any migration; dual-write period ≥ 1 billing cycle
model_routing_config
Low (config writes)
Controls traffic routing; bad migration silently misroutes
Test rollback in staging under load before production
rate_limit_state
Very high (per-request counters)
Redis, not RDBMS — migration is Redis key migration
Shadow-copy pattern: write to both old and new key during transition
eval_results
Medium (async eval jobs)
Append-only; data loss is recoverable from re-running evals
Standard Alembic migration acceptable; eval re-run is rollback plan
Summary

Data migration rollback plans begin with classification: trivially reversible migrations have a DROP rollback measured in seconds; constrained migrations have a down script measured in minutes; irreversible migrations require a pre-migration snapshot with a restore TTR of 15–60 minutes and a data-loss window that must be quantified and accepted before proceeding. The expand/contract pattern avoids irreversibility entirely by splitting breaking changes into three non-breaking phases. Test every rollback plan on a production clone before the migration runs in production — rollback procedures that are only tested on staging data will fail in production at the worst possible time.
Classify every migration before writing it: trivial / expand-contract / constrained / irreversible
Expand/contract splits schema changes into backward-compatible phases; rollback before contract is always safe
Alembic down migrations handle constrained reversals; run pre-downgrade safety checks for data loss risk
Irreversible migrations require a dedicated pre-migration RDS snapshot (not the daily backup)
Test rollback on production clone; measure actual TTR; update runbook if TTR exceeds target
Claude API billing and conversation_logs tables require expand/contract — never ADD NOT NULL without a default on high-write tables
